script:
Good evening, I’m Claire. Here’s your international news roundup.

We begin in Africa, where President Donald Trump says a joint U.S.-Nigerian operation has killed a senior figure in the Islamic State group. In a late-night post on social media, Trump said Abu Bakr al-Mainuki was the group’s second in command globally. Officials have not yet released full operational details, but the announcement adds a dramatic new turn to the long-running fight against extremist networks across the Sahel and beyond. For Washington and Abuja, the strike will likely be presented as a sign of cooperation. For security analysts, the key questions now are whether the claim holds up under independent verification, and what retaliation, if any, might follow.

In the Middle East, Israel says it has killed the leader of Hamas’ military wing, describing him as one of the architects of the October 7th attacks that triggered the war in Gaza. If confirmed, this would be one of the most significant Israeli strikes in months. But the broader picture remains grim. The war continues to exact a heavy toll on civilians, with fresh reports of destruction and displacement across Gaza. Each military gain appears to come with another layer of humanitarian suffering, and there is still no clear path to a ceasefire that could bring lasting relief to people trapped in the conflict.

Meanwhile, diplomacy involving Iran remains tense and complicated. Iran’s foreign minister says a lack of trust is still the biggest obstacle in negotiations with the United States over the country’s nuclear program. That’s a familiar problem, but one that carries fresh urgency amid the wider instability across the region. Every round of talks is shaped by deep suspicion, regional rivalries, and the ever-present fear that diplomacy could collapse into confrontation. For now, the message from Tehran is that even if talks continue, progress will be slow, fragile, and hard-earned.

In Europe, all eyes are on Vienna, where the Eurovision Song Contest has reached its grand final. The atmosphere is, as ever, part celebration and part political storm. Fans have turned out in force despite rainy weather and tight security, but critics are also making their voices heard, particularly over Israel’s participation in this year’s competition. Eurovision has always sold itself as a joyful escape, a place where pop music, glitter, and spectacle take center stage. But in 2026, the contest is once again a mirror of Europe’s deeper divisions. It is hard to miss the contrast between the sequins on stage and the protests outside.

And that tension is part of what makes Eurovision endure. It is never just about the songs. It is about identity, belonging, and the strange power of a television broadcast to become a battleground for politics, culture, and national pride. This year’s grand final promises all the usual ingredients: bold performances, dramatic staging, and the possibility of a surprise winner. But the mood in Vienna suggests that, even in a competition built on unity, Europe’s fractures are never far from the surface.

In Germany, a different kind of transatlantic tension is unfolding. President Trump’s threat to withdraw U.S. troops has rattled residents of a small Bavarian town that has long depended on the American military presence. For decades, U.S. service members and their families have been part of the local fabric there — not just a source of income, but of friendships, school ties, and daily life. Now, people are worried about what could happen if those troops leave. It is a reminder that foreign policy decisions made in Washington can have deeply personal consequences thousands of miles away.

And beyond the headlines, there is a growing concern that will affect every region on the map: extreme fire outbreaks are reaching record levels around the world. Reuters reports that climate change has driven unprecedented fire activity in Africa, Asia, and elsewhere this year, with conditions expected to worsen. That means more dangerous seasons ahead, more pressure on emergency services, and more communities forced to live with the threat of fire as a constant reality rather than a rare disaster. It is one more sign that climate change is no longer a distant warning. It is here, it is escalating, and it is reshaping daily life.

So tonight’s international picture is one of conflict, uncertainty, and performance under pressure. From counterterrorism in Africa, to war in Gaza, to delicate diplomacy with Iran, to the spectacle and politics of Eurovision, the world feels both connected and unsettled. And as always, the stories are not just about governments and armies and institutions. They are about people — the families in Gaza, the residents in Bavaria, the fans in Vienna, and the communities facing a hotter, harsher climate.

I’m Claire. Thanks for listening.

sujet: BBC Radio 5 Sports Extra: why live sport still wins listeners
script:
[Yanis] Bonjour à tous, welcome to the show. Today’s debate is simple, but it goes straight to the heart of modern sports media: in a world full of clips, streams, highlights, and social feeds, does live radio coverage still matter?

[Emma] I’m going to say yes, absolutely. And the numbers back that up. BBC Radio 5 Live reached 5.4 million weekly listeners, and 5 Live Sports Extra climbed to 907,000 listeners last quarter, up 56% year on year. That is not a niche audience. That is a sign that live sport on radio is still deeply relevant.

[Yanis] Or it’s a sign that people turn to radio when they can’t get the full visual product. I’m not dismissing it, but let’s be honest: if fans had every match on TV or a perfect stream, would they still choose radio in such numbers?

[Emma] I think they would, because radio offers something TV doesn’t always deliver. Imagination. Pace. Personality. A good commentator can make a match feel bigger than the screen. And when there’s a packed sporting schedule, as BBC said there was, listeners want instant reaction, context, and companionship.

[Yanis] Companionship is a nice word, but sport is about action. The argument against radio is that it is inherently secondary. It follows the event rather than leading it. It depends on the game being elsewhere, on another platform.

[Emma] Secondary doesn’t mean inferior. It means different. And in sport, different is valuable. Think about fans driving, working, cooking, or commuting. They cannot watch every minute, but they can follow every moment. Radio keeps them connected in real time.

[Yanis] Yet the modern fan is used to choice. They want data, multi-angle replays, tactical analysis, and instant clips. Radio gives you emotion, yes, but not the full picture. So the question becomes: is radio essential, or simply convenient?

[Emma] I’d argue it’s essential in the sense that it reaches people other media miss. BBC’s overall audio offer had an average weekly audience of 5.8 million across BBC platforms, with a record peak of 7.2 million during The Traitors: Uncloaked finale. That tells you audio is still powerful. And sports audio is part of that ecosystem.

[Yanis] But that broader audio success also raises a challenge. People are listening to podcasts, on-demand shows, and visualised content. So maybe sports radio survives because it has evolved, not because traditional live commentary is unbeatable.

[Emma] Evolution is exactly the point. Sports radio is not frozen in time. It now sits alongside podcasts, clips, analysis, and digital streaming. BBC Sport, BBC News, and on-demand radio are all part of the same habit. Live sport is the anchor that still draws people in.

[Yanis] I’ll concede that live moments matter. Big goals, late wickets, final holes, last-lap drama — those are made for radio. But for regular-season sport, I’m not convinced the audience is emotionally attached to the format. I think they’re attached to the event.

[Emma] And the event is what radio serves best in the moment. That’s why BBC Radio 5 Live is still so strong during major sporting and news cycles. It’s fast, it’s immediate, and it feels alive. You don’t just hear the score — you hear the tension.

[Yanis] Let’s talk about accessibility, because that’s one of radio’s strongest points. No subscription barrier, no complex setup, no need to stare at a screen. In that sense, radio is democratic. It levels the playing field.

[Emma] Exactly. And for many fans, that matters more than people admit. Not everyone wants the noise of a studio panel or the overload of a digital app. Sometimes you want one voice, one match, one narrative. Sports Extra is built for that.

[Yanis] Still, I’d push back on the idea that radio is growing because people prefer it. The 56% rise in Sports Extra could reflect an unusually busy calendar. More events, more tune-ins, more opportunities. That doesn’t automatically mean long-term loyalty.

[Emma] Maybe, but that’s true of all media performance. The real test is whether audiences return. And they do. BBC Radio 4 remains the UK’s leading speech station with 9.15 million weekly listeners, while 5 Live holds a major position in news and sport. That suggests habit, not just hype.

[Yanis] Habit is strong, I agree. But sports audiences are increasingly fragmented. Some want live commentary, some want social debate, some want statistical breakdowns. Radio is one part of a larger fan journey, not the whole journey.

[Emma] And that’s fine. It doesn’t need to be the whole journey. It needs to be indispensable at the moments that matter. When a match swings, when a title race tightens, when a national team plays, radio becomes the soundtrack of shared emotion.

[Yanis] Shared emotion — that’s the strongest case for it. Because even if you’re alone in the car or on the train, you feel part of something bigger. That communal feeling is rare in modern media.

[Emma] And that’s why live sport on radio still wins. Not because it replaces TV, not because it competes with every app, but because it gives fans immediate access to drama, analysis, and atmosphere without asking them to stop their lives.

[Yanis] So maybe the verdict is this: radio is not the future of sport in the exclusive sense, but it remains one of the most resilient ways to experience it.

[Emma] I’ll take that. Resilient, accessible, emotional, and still growing where it counts. In a crowded media market, that’s a serious achievement.

[Yanis] And on that note, we’ll leave it there. The debate continues, but one thing is clear: live sports radio is far from out of the game.

[Emma] Thanks for listening, and join us next time for another debate on the stories shaping sport today.

sujet: Tech & AI: the week automation got real
script:
[Marc] Bonjour à tous, and welcome back. Today, we’re looking at a tech week that feels like a turning point. AI is no longer just chatting, coding, or generating images. It’s entering legal work, reshaping phones, changing cars, and, yes, shaking up jobs and budgets.

[Léa] Exactly. And the big story is not one single product launch. It’s the pattern. AI is moving from “helpful tool” to “agentic system” — meaning software that can take actions, not just answer questions. That sounds exciting, but it also raises a lot of practical questions.

[Marc] Let’s start with the legal world, because that one really stands out. Anthropic is reportedly pushing into AI legal services, with tools that can help with document review and drafting. On paper, that’s a dream for efficiency.

[Léa] But legal work is also a trust business. If an AI drafts a contract clause or summarizes a case, who is responsible when something is missed? And maybe even more importantly: if the software saves time, does it really save money for the client in the long run, or does it just change where the bill appears?

[Marc] That’s the paradox, isn’t it? AI often lowers the cost of doing one task, but the market can respond by raising expectations, increasing volume, or shifting the work elsewhere. So efficiency doesn’t always mean affordability.

[Léa] Right. And we’re seeing that same tension across the industry. Companies are investing heavily in AI infrastructure while also cutting jobs. Reuters has been tracking firms tapping debt markets to fund AI and cloud expansion. Meta, for example, has been raising huge sums to support its AI push.

[Marc] And AP News reported that big companies like Meta, Microsoft, Amazon, and Block are cutting thousands of jobs while simultaneously investing billions in AI. That combination is hard to ignore.

[Léa] It sends a very clear message to workers: the company may be growing, but not necessarily in headcount. Automation is becoming part of the business model, not just a side project.

[Marc] And that brings us to the emotional side of this story. Because when people hear “AI efficiency,” they often hear “fewer roles.” Even when companies say the goal is to reconfigure teams or become more productive, the workforce hears risk.

[Léa] Especially when the language gets very direct. Meta’s CEO has said that 2026 is the year AI starts to dramatically change how we work. That’s not a subtle hint. That’s a roadmap.

[Marc] Now, to be fair, not all of the news is about layoffs and cost pressure. Some of it is genuinely about making everyday products smarter. Google, for instance, is integrating Gemini into more of its ecosystem, including things like dictation in Gboard.

[Léa] Which sounds small, but it matters. If your keyboard can understand you better, suggest better text, and help you move faster, that’s AI becoming invisible infrastructure. It stops being a destination and becomes part of the background.

[Marc] And Google’s Android announcements are very much in that spirit. There’s this idea of “vibe-coded widgets” and a tool called “Create My Widget,” where you can describe what you want in natural language, and the system helps build it.

[Léa] That’s a strong sign of where consumer software is headed. Instead of learning menus, layers, and settings, users describe intent. “I want a meal-prep dashboard,” “I want a workout widget,” “I want a calmer home screen.” The interface becomes conversational.

[Marc] But then Google also added something more human: Android 17’s Pause Point feature, designed to combat doomscrolling by enforcing breaks before opening distracting apps.

[Léa] I actually love that idea. It’s one of the few examples where AI-adjacent design is not just about doing more, but about helping us do less. Or at least helping us be more intentional.

[Marc] Though there’s a philosophical question there too: should your phone be the thing that tells you to stop using your phone?

[Léa] Fair point. But if the machine is already optimized to keep us engaged, maybe a little counter-optimization is overdue.

[Marc] Another major hardware angle is Google’s new AI-native laptops, reportedly built for Gemini intelligence from the ground up. The pitch is not just “faster laptop,” but a device designed around proactive assistance and seamless AI integration.

[Léa] That’s a huge shift in product philosophy. We used to buy laptops to run software. Now the hardware itself is being shaped around an AI layer that anticipates tasks, summarizes information, and maybe even orchestrates work across apps.

[Marc] And on the security side, Android’s Advanced Protection Mode is getting features like Intrusion Logging, aimed especially at activists and journalists who may be targeted by spyware.

[Léa] That’s important because AI doesn’t only create new capabilities for creators and consumers — it also creates new capabilities for attackers. So every leap forward in convenience has to be matched by stronger defenses.

[Marc] Security is a great bridge to another trend this week: companies are not only building AI, they’re training people to use it securely. Secure Code Warrior partnering with AWS to launch AI learning modules is a good example.

[Léa] Absolutely. The narrative is shifting from “learn AI tools” to “learn AI tools safely.” Developers need to understand not just how to prompt a model, but how to avoid leaking data, introducing vulnerabilities, or trusting outputs too much.

[Marc] And that’s where agentic AI gets tricky. If a system can chain actions across tools, it can also chain mistakes. One wrong assumption can cascade through multiple steps.

[Léa] Which is why a lot of companies are trying to build guardrails into the workflow. Red Hat’s expanded developer suite is part of that story too — bridging local development and cloud deployment for agentic AI. The goal is to move from experiment to production without losing control.

[Marc] That’s the enterprise dream: build fast, deploy safely, scale confidently.

[Léa] But the enterprise reality is often more complicated. You need governance, logging, permissions, audit trails, and human oversight. Otherwise, “agentic” can quickly become “unpredictable.”

[Marc] Meanwhile, the startup ecosystem is still very much alive. A Capital, led by Kevin Hartz, closed a $450 million fund focused on AI, fintech, healthcare, and security. That tells us investors still see enormous opportunity.

[Léa] They do — and they’re betting on a broad AI economy, not just model companies. The next wave may be applications, workflow tools, compliance systems, and sector-specific products.

[Marc] Which makes sense, because the value may increasingly be in how AI is embedded, not in the model alone.

[Léa] Exactly. And if you look at the broader market, there’s a real race to own the user layer. Meta is reportedly building a highly personalized AI assistant. Google is weaving Gemini into products people already use. Apple is being pushed toward a more open AI ecosystem. Everyone wants to be the default interface.

[Marc] And the competition is not just technical, it’s behavioral. Whoever becomes the assistant people trust every day will have a massive strategic advantage.

[Léa] But that also means the stakes around privacy, transparency, and regulation keep rising. We’re seeing more discussion about labeling AI-generated content, disclosure requirements, and compliance obligations, especially in places like California and the EU.

[Marc] Because once AI becomes part of everyday work and media, people need to know what’s synthetic, what’s assisted, and what’s fully human-made.

[Léa] And regulators are clearly catching up. The message is: if you’re deploying AI at scale, you can’t treat provenance and labeling as an afterthought anymore.

[Marc] So, if we step back, what’s the big takeaway from this week?

[Léa] For me, it’s that AI has crossed a threshold. It’s not just generating content. It’s entering workflows, devices, operating systems, vehicles, legal services, and business operations. That makes it more useful — and more consequential.

[Marc] And it means the conversation has to mature too. We can’t just ask, “What can AI do?” We also have to ask, “Who benefits, who is accountable, what gets automated, and what gets protected?”

[Léa] That’s the real story. The future of AI isn’t one big breakthrough. It’s a thousand small integrations that slowly change how we work, buy, drive, code, and even scroll.

[Marc] And maybe, just maybe, a few of those integrations will help us slow down instead of speed up.

[Léa] From your keyboard to your car to your widget dashboard, AI is becoming the operating layer of modern life.

[Marc] That’s it for today. Stay curious, stay critical, and don’t let the agent make all the decisions.

[Léa] And if your phone asks you to take a Pause Point, maybe listen.

[Marc] Thanks for listening, and we’ll be back soon with more on the technologies shaping tomorrow.

sujet: Culture in 2026: big-name exhibitions, bold music returns, and who gets to set the agenda
script:
[Sofia] Bonjour à tous, and welcome back. Today’s debate is simple on the surface, but it gets complicated fast: in 2026, what actually drives culture? Is it the blockbuster exhibition, the comeback album, the festival circuit, the institutions, or the communities creating work outside the spotlight?

[Hugo] And the answer, of course, is yes to all of it. But if we’re being honest, the cultural calendar for 2026 is already telling us something interesting: attention is split. We’ve got major museums, major venues, major artists, and at the same time a growing appetite for work that feels intimate, local, and even a little rebellious.

[Sofia] Exactly. Let’s start with the big-ticket names. Björk taking over the National Gallery of Iceland is not just an exhibition announcement. It’s a statement. Her project, Echolalia, is described as an immersive installation linked to a new album in development. That is so Björk: music, visual art, technology, performance, and atmosphere all collapsing into one experience.

[Hugo] And that’s why people travel for this kind of thing. It’s not just “go see an exhibition.” It’s “go step inside an artist’s brain.” Björk has always worked in that borderland between genres. The question is whether institutions are now using artists like her to stay culturally relevant, or whether the artists are using the institutions to expand the definition of what a gallery can be.

[Sofia] I’d argue both. But it raises a bigger cultural issue: are museums becoming experience engines? Because once you have immersive installations, you’re not only preserving art, you’re staging it. And that can be thrilling, but it can also become a bit of a spectacle economy.

[Hugo] True, although spectacle isn’t automatically shallow. Sometimes spectacle is the gateway. Someone goes for Björk and leaves thinking differently about sound, space, and visual storytelling. That’s not a bad outcome. And frankly, culture has always needed its magnets.

[Sofia] Fair. But let’s move to another magnet: Salman Toor at The Courtauld in London, his first show in Europe. That one feels significant for a different reason. Toor’s paintings are lush, intimate, and emotionally loaded, portraying queer Brown men with old-master technique. This is not just visibility; it’s canon-making.

[Hugo] Yes, and that’s where the debate gets more serious. When an artist like Toor enters a major European institution, the question isn’t only “is this a good show?” It’s “who gets to be placed in dialogue with the history of painting?” Because the old canon was never neutral. It was curated by power.

[Sofia] Exactly. And Toor’s work is powerful because it doesn’t ask permission. It enters that lineage and rewrites the terms. That’s cultural progress in action: not replacing the canon with something flimsy, but forcing the canon to confront itself.

[Hugo] Though some critics will always say the institution is simply catching up. Which may be true. But catching up is still movement. And if 2026 is shaping up as a year of major exhibitions, then maybe the real story is that audiences now expect institutions to do more than archive. They want them to intervene.

[Sofia] That expectation also shows up in the music world. Look at the albums and releases lined up for May 2026: The All-American Rejects returning after 14 years, Ed O’Brien with Blue Morpho, Future Islands, Kacey Musgraves, The Black Keys, American Football. That’s a lot of nostalgia, but not lazy nostalgia. It’s legacy acts trying to prove they still have something to say.

[Hugo] And that matters because music culture in the streaming era often feels disposable. Albums can get swallowed by the algorithm. So when artists with a history come back, they’re not just releasing songs; they’re asking for sustained attention. They’re saying, “Sit with this.”

[Sofia] But does the public still do that? That’s the challenge. We live in a culture of fragments. One week it’s a new album, the next it’s a festival clip, then a viral snippet, then it disappears. So a 2026 album release only becomes culturally meaningful if people are willing to listen beyond the headline.

[Hugo] I think some audiences still are. Look at the appeal of Future Islands, for instance. Their music is built for emotional release, for performance, for the kind of live experience that feels larger than a feed. And Ed O’Brien’s solo work, by description, leans atmospheric and environmental. That’s not background music. That’s music asking to be inhabited.

[Sofia] Which brings us to a broader point: culture in 2026 seems to be splitting into two desires. On one side, there’s the giant, immersive, institution-backed experience. On the other, there’s the deeply personal, often community-rooted work that wants to create meaning without scale.

[Hugo] And that second category is where the future may actually be. Take the arts residencies in Inverclyde, Scotland, tied to the Galoshans Festival. They’re supporting socially engaged work rooted in local storytelling, seasonal celebration, and community traditions. That’s not glamorous in the way a museum takeover is glamorous. But it may be more culturally durable.

[Sofia] I agree. Because community-based art asks different questions. Not “How many tickets did it sell?” but “Who did it include?” Not “Did it trend?” but “Did it belong?” In an era where culture can feel overproduced, that kind of rootedness is invaluable.

[Hugo] Although let’s not pretend community work is automatically pure. It still has to navigate funding, access, and visibility. The difference is that the stakes are often more honest. You can’t hide behind branding as easily when you’re working face to face with people.

[Sofia] That honesty is also what makes certain literary and archival cultural moments resonate. I’m thinking of Sandra Cisneros reflecting on meeting Robert Duvall and being too flummoxed to dance with him. It’s a tiny anecdote, but it says a lot about culture as lived experience. Sometimes culture isn’t the grand stage. It’s the awkward, human moment you carry forever.

[Hugo] Beautifully put. And that’s why I think debates about culture often go wrong when they only focus on prestige. Prestige matters, yes, but culture is also memory, embarrassment, desire, and chance. A dance invitation, a concert, a film premiere, a gallery opening — all of it becomes part of how people narrate their lives.

[Sofia] So let me push back a little. If culture is all of that, then why do we still obsess so much over the major institutions and major names? Why does Björk’s gallery project feel bigger than a thousand smaller, equally meaningful events?

[Hugo] Because scale changes the conversation. A major name can open doors that smaller artists have been knocking on for years. Björk at the National Gallery of Iceland gets press. Salman Toor at The Courtauld gets international attention. Those moments shift the symbolic center.

[Sofia] But do they shift it enough? Or do they simply decorate the center while the periphery remains underfunded?

[Hugo] That’s the uncomfortable question. The truth is, large cultural events can inspire, but they can also absorb oxygen. When every headline is about a celebrity artist or a museum takeover, it’s easy to miss the local gallery, the community residency, the experimental festival, the writer reading to twenty people who will remember it for years.

[Sofia] And yet, the big events can function as gateways. Someone drawn in by Björk may discover contemporary Icelandic art. Someone visiting Toor may start following younger queer painters. Someone lured by a pop-rock comeback may end up exploring the broader indie scene again.

[Hugo] So maybe the real debate isn’t big versus small. It’s whether the ecosystem is connected enough that the big events feed the rest, instead of replacing them.

[Sofia] I like that. A healthy culture should have a circulatory system. The major institutions circulate attention, resources, and legitimacy. The grassroots scene circulates risk, experimentation, and authenticity. If one side stops, the whole thing weakens.

[Hugo] And 2026, at least from this calendar, looks like a year where both sides are visible. We’ve got museum-scale ambition, first-in-Europe exhibitions, album comebacks, live audio festivals, residencies, documentary events, and local art calendars that keep communities alive.

[Sofia] That’s the hopeful reading. The less hopeful reading is that culture is becoming more and more dependent on eventization: everything must be a moment, an activation, a limited run, a takeover, a launch.

[Hugo] But culture has always been event-driven to some extent. The difference now is the speed. The pressure is to keep producing “moments.” That can be exhausting for artists and audiences alike.

[Sofia] Which is why I think the most radical cultural act in 2026 may be slowness. A gallery visit that takes hours. An album listened to from beginning to end. A residency that builds relationships over weeks. A festival that leaves room for conversation, not just consumption.

[Hugo] Agreed. And maybe that’s the real answer to our opening question. What drives culture in 2026? Not just fame, not just institutions, not just community. It’s the tension between scale and depth. Between what gets announced and what gets remembered.

[Sofia] And between who gets invited in, and who gets to define the room in the first place.

[Hugo] Well said. If 2026’s cultural calendar is any indication, the best work will be the work that doesn’t just entertain us, but reorganizes our sense of where culture lives.

[Sofia] On that note, we’ll leave it there. Whether you’re heading to a gallery, pressing play on a long-awaited album, or supporting a local residency, the real challenge is to stay curious.

[Hugo] And to pay attention beyond the headline.

[Sofia] Thanks for listening.

[Hugo] We’ll be back soon with more debate, more culture, and hopefully fewer empty buzzwords.

[Sofia] No promises.

[Hugo] That’s fair.

sujet:
Wellness in 2026
From solo optimization to shared experiences
script:
[Tom] Bonjour à tous, and welcome back to the show. Today we’re talking lifestyle, but not in the usual “how to drink more water and get eight hours of sleep” way. We’re looking at where wellness is headed in 2026, and honestly, it feels like the whole idea is getting a reboot.

[Inès] Oui, exactly. The big shift is that wellness is moving away from being this lonely, hyper-optimized project. It’s becoming more social, more personal, and, in a funny way, more human. Less “perfect routine,” more “what actually fits your life.”

[Tom] That’s the key phrase, isn’t it? What fits your life. Because for years, wellness has often sounded like a second job. Track your steps, count your protein, monitor your sleep, optimize your breathing, optimize your recovery, optimize your recovery from optimizing. At some point, you wonder: are we feeling better, or just busier?

[Inès] And 2026 seems to be answering that question with a big no to the burnout model. One of the strongest trends is personalization. Not a retreat where everyone wakes up at 6 a.m., does the same yoga flow, eats the same salad, and pretends to love it. The new approach is about tailoring programs to each person’s body, mind, and lifestyle.

[Tom] That makes so much sense, especially in travel. People don’t want to pause their wellness journey when they go away. They want to keep training, recover properly, maybe learn something new, and come home with habits they can actually use on Monday morning.

[Inès] Exactly. The wellness trip is no longer just a pampering break. It’s becoming a kind of life lab. You go somewhere beautiful, yes, but you also test what works for you. Maybe it’s boxing in the morning, pilates in the afternoon, a medical assessment, a nutrition reset, or simply learning how to build a routine that doesn’t collapse the minute you get back to real life.

[Tom] And that’s where the word “bespoke” keeps popping up. Retreats are starting to look less like fixed schedules and more like a conversation. Before you even arrive, they may look at your health data, your goals, your stress levels, your habits, and then build a journey around that.

[Inès] Which is very 2026, because people are also getting suspicious of one-size-fits-all advice. One supplement for everyone? No. One morning routine for everyone? Also no. We’re seeing more customized nutrition, more specialized supplementation, and more people asking what their own body actually needs instead of copying what’s trending on social media.

[Tom] Speaking of social media, it’s interesting that while wellness has been very visible online, the next wave seems to be pushing back against the screen. Digital detox is becoming a major theme. People are craving analogue habits again: journaling on paper, writing letters, taking long walks without a podcast in their ears, even using dumb phones.

[Inès] Oui, and that doesn’t mean anti-tech in an extreme way. It’s more about balance. Tech can help with recovery, diagnostics, and training, but wellness in 2026 is also about nervous-system safety. That phrase is everywhere now. People want less pressure, less performance language, less constant measurement.

[Tom] So instead of “How many calories did you burn?” it becomes “How do you feel?”

[Inès] Precisely. And that’s a big cultural move. Wellness is shifting from metrics to mood, from output to presence. Even the language around fitness is softening. It’s less about crushing it, more about showing up. Less hustle, more harmony.

[Tom] And yet, it’s not all quiet breathing and herbal tea. There’s another big trend: movement as community. That’s huge. Run clubs, sauna gatherings, group ice baths, Pilates parties, even destination marathons and endurance trips. People are planning travel around shared physical goals.

[Inès] That part is really interesting, because for a long time wellness was marketed as solo self-improvement. But now, the social side is back. Healing happens together. People want to sweat together, recover together, and celebrate together. Community is becoming part of the medicine.

[Tom] I love that idea. Because let’s be honest, it’s easier to stay motivated when you’re not doing it alone. A run club is not just about running. It’s about seeing the same faces every week, having a reason to get out the door, and maybe grabbing coffee afterward.

[Inès] And there’s something deeper too. Group rituals are coming back in a big way. Things like sweat-lodge ceremonies, shared breathwork, somatic dance, silent raves, even grief raves. Wellness is becoming expressive. It’s not only about calm. It’s about release, connection, and sometimes catharsis.

[Tom] That’s such a good point. For years, wellness was almost too polished. White towels, eucalyptus, perfect lighting. Now there’s room for the messy stuff: emotion, joy, shaking it out, crying in a circle, laughing in a sauna. It feels less curated in a fake way, more real in a meaningful way.

[Inès] And that realness is also showing up in what people want from travel. Instead of just lying by a pool and calling it recovery, they want contrast therapy, cold plunges, saunas, steam, water, air, heat. They want to feel something. The body is part of the experience again.

[Tom] Contrast therapy is one of those trends that sounds intense but is actually pretty simple: alternate between hot and cold, and let the body respond. It’s popular because people like the idea of something elemental, almost primal. Heat, cold, breath, movement. No fancy language needed.

[Inès] Yes, and that elemental quality is important. In a world of endless digital noise, people are looking for experiences that bring them back into their senses. A cold plunge is brutally honest. A sauna is brutally honest. You can’t multitask your way through it.

[Tom] Which may be why low-stimulation retreats are also gaining attention. Not every wellness experience needs a packed itinerary and a hundred activities. Sometimes the luxury is fewer decisions, less noise, and more space to just be.

[Inès] And that ties into another trend: home as the new wellness center. We used to think of wellness as something you go out to find. Now people are designing homes, kitchens, bedrooms, even lighting, to support longevity and recovery. Wellness is moving into everyday life.

[Tom] So instead of chasing a perfect retreat once a year, the idea is to build small habits into daily routines. Wellness stacking is a great example. You pair a healthy habit with something you already do: stretch while brushing your teeth, drink water when the alarm goes off, breathe deeply while the kettle boils.

[Inès] Simple, practical, and very realistic. That’s probably why it’s catching on. It doesn’t ask people to become a different person. It just helps them become a slightly better version of the person they already are.

[Tom] And maybe that’s the real headline for 2026 wellness. It’s not about becoming extreme. It’s about becoming sustainable.

[Inès] Oui, sustainable and enjoyable. Pleasure is back in the conversation too. Wellness is no longer only discipline and restriction. It’s also joy, softness, presence, and connection. That’s a huge difference.

[Tom] So if we had to sum up the year ahead in one sentence, I’d say this: wellness in 2026 is less about chasing perfection and more about building a life that actually feels good to live.

[Inès] I love that. Because in the end, the best wellness habit is the one you can keep. Not for a week, not for a holiday, but for real life.

[Tom] And real life, thankfully, includes friends, movement, rest, laughter, and the occasional digital detox.

[Inès] Plus maybe a sauna, a run club, and a retreat that actually knows your name.

[Tom] That sounds like a very good 2026 to me.

[Inès] Absolutely. Stay with us, because after the break we’re talking about the everyday habits people are using to make all this wellness actually stick.

[Tom] Don’t go anywhere.

sujet: Sport Today: the games, the calls, and the voices behind them
script:
If you want to know what sport sounds like right now, listen to the numbers, listen to the noise, and listen to the questions being asked by fans. Because this week, the story isn’t just about who won, who lost, or who’s top of the table. It’s also about how people are following sport, and how they want to understand it.

Let’s start with the bigger picture. BBC Radio has had a strong start to 2026, and sport is a big part of that. BBC Radio 5 Live reached 5.4 million weekly listeners, while 5 Live Sports Extra climbed to 907,000 listeners last quarter, up 56% on the same period last year. That’s a huge jump, and it tells you something very simple: when the sporting calendar is full, audiences show up. They want live voices, live reaction, and live analysis. They want to feel close to the action, whether it’s football, golf, tennis, cricket, or the endless stream of major events that keep the sports world moving.

And that appetite goes beyond live commentary. The BBC says its audio across BBC Sport, BBC News, BBC Bitesize and visualised podcasts on iPlayer reached an average weekly audience of 5.8 million, with a record peak of 7.2 million during The Traitors: Uncloaked finale. That may not sound like a sports headline at first glance, but it matters. It shows the scale of audio as a habit. People are comfortable turning to radio and on-demand listening for clarity, speed, and personality. In sport, that matters even more, because the best coverage doesn’t just tell you the score. It helps you understand the moment.

That’s exactly where BBC Radio 5 Sports Extra comes in. On a day like today, its promise is straightforward: more live commentaries from the world’s greatest sporting events. And for fans, “more” is not a small word. More cameras, more angles, more pundits, more podcasts, more tactical breakdowns, more reaction clips, more streams. Modern sport is not consumed in one neat sitting anymore. It’s layered. It’s live, then replayed, then clipped, then discussed, then argued over, sometimes for days.

And if you want proof that sport is still driving conversation, look at the schedule. In the United States, baseball is in full flow, with the latest headlines dominated by series openers, injuries, bullpen battles, and the kind of nightly drama that keeps MLB fans checking scores constantly. The Yankees, Mets, Phillies, Padres, Cubs, Rangers, Reds, Guardians, Mariners, Blue Jays, and more are all part of a packed slate. That’s the beauty of baseball this time of year: there’s always another game, another adjustment, another injury update, another chance to shift momentum. One night you’re celebrating a big win; the next, you’re trying to figure out what a bruised elbow, a viral illness, or a cold streak means for the next two weeks.

Elsewhere, the PGA Championship is commanding attention too. BBC Radio 5 Live has been covering the event with golf correspondent Iain Carter alongside Oli Wilson and Trish Johnson, which is exactly the kind of expert mix that makes audio work so well in golf. Golf is a sport where the pauses matter just as much as the shots. You need people who can explain the swing, the pressure, the course conditions, and the psychology. And right now, with a major championship in progress, every round has the potential to reshape the leaderboard and the conversation.

Then there’s the Preakness Stakes, another reminder that sport isn’t one thing. It’s not just football and cricket and tennis. It’s horse racing, too, with its own rhythm, its own audience, and its own traditions. One of the reasons sports radio remains so powerful is that it can move across all of these worlds without missing a beat. One minute you’re hearing about a golf major, the next it’s baseball, then racing, then football, then a breaking story from the NFL or the wider sporting business.

And speaking of the sporting business, one of the most interesting developments right now isn’t a transfer, a title race, or a championship run. It’s transparency. ESPN has highlighted how the UFL is experimenting with in-game referee interviews, giving viewers a direct explanation for decisions in real time. That may sound small, but it could be one of the most significant shifts in modern sport media. For years, fans have shouted the same thing at their screens: “Why wasn’t that a foul?” “Why was that overturned?” “What did the referee see?”

Now, in the UFL, the answer is being brought into the broadcast. Not every time, and not for every call, but enough to change the tone of the conversation. Officials are explaining decisions. Broadcasters are airing those explanations. And that “why” is becoming part of the product. Whether fans agree or not, they’re being brought inside the process. That’s important, because a lot of modern sport is really about trust. Trust in the rules. Trust in the competition. Trust that the people making the decisions are accountable enough to explain them.

It also reflects a bigger trend across sport media: audiences don’t just want polished highlight packages anymore. They want access. They want the human layer. They want the argument, the uncertainty, the context. That’s why live radio still matters. It can handle nuance in a way that a short clip often can’t. A commentator can pause, explain, correct, and widen the lens. A good sports presenter can make a messy call feel understandable, even when it remains controversial.

And that brings us back to BBC Radio 5 Live Sports Extra, because its growth suggests there’s still a strong market for that kind of coverage. In an era when everything is available on demand, live sport still has a special pull. People want to know what’s happening now. They want to hear the crowd, the tension, the reaction. They want to share the experience with other fans, even if they’re listening alone in the car, at work, or on the train.

The same is true for the broader BBC sports offer. When a packed news and sports agenda pushes audiences toward speech radio, it reinforces something broadcasters have known for decades: sport is one of the last truly live communal experiences. Even if you’re not in the stadium, you can still feel part of it. That’s the magic of a great commentary team, a sharp reporter, or a presenter who knows when to let the moment breathe.

So what do we take from all this? First, sport remains one of the most powerful drivers of audience attention. Second, fans are consuming it in more ways than ever: live radio, podcasts, streams, clips, and on-demand audio. And third, the future of sports coverage may be less about hiding the process and more about opening it up.

Whether it’s a baseball pennant race, a golf major, a horse race at Pimlico, or a referee explaining a call under the lights, the message is the same. Fans don’t just want the result. They want the story behind it. They want the voice in their ear, the explanation in the moment, and the feeling that they’re part of the conversation.

And if the latest audience figures are anything to go by, that conversation is only getting bigger.

sujet: Bilan de la journée : retour sur les événements marquants
script: Bonsoir, il est 18 heures. Je suis Claire pour le journal du soir sur AI Radio. La journée a été marquée par les retombées du sommet du G7 et les premières réactions des marchés financiers. En Europe, le Parlement européen a adopté en première lecture une directive sur la régulation de l'intelligence artificielle dans les services publics. Et du côté du sport, les demis de finale de la Ligue des Champions ont livré leur lot de surprises. Voici le détail.

sujet: Open source vs propriétaire : le grand débat de l'IA en 2026
script: Bonsoir, je suis Marc. Ce soir, je vous propose de revenir sur un débat qui agite le monde de la tech depuis plusieurs mois : faut-il que les modèles d'IA soient open source ou rester propriétaires ? D'un côté, des acteurs comme Meta, Mistral ou DeepSeek plaident pour la transparence et l'accessibilité. Ils arguent que des modèles ouverts permettent à la communauté de détecter les biais, d'auditer la sécurité et de démocratiser l'accès. De l'autre côté, OpenAI, Anthropic et Google estiment que certains systèmes sont trop puissants pour être diffusés sans garde-fous. Le risque de détournement est réel. La question n'a pas de réponse simple, mais une chose est certaine : le choix que nous faisons collectivement aujourd'hui dessinera l'IA de demain.

sujet: L'art généré par IA mérite-t-il d'être exposé dans les musées ?
script:
[Sofia] Bonsoir à tous. Ce soir, Hugo et moi allons débattre d'une question qui fait rage dans le monde de l'art : l'art généré par intelligence artificielle a-t-il sa place dans les musées ?
[Hugo] Je vais défendre le oui. Un musée doit être le miroir de son époque. Et notre époque, c'est celle de l'IA. Exclure ces œuvres, c'est nier une réalité culturelle majeure.
[Sofia] Je comprends l'argument, mais je ne suis pas d'accord. Ce qui fait la valeur d'une œuvre d'art, c'est l'intention, la vulnérabilité, le vécu de l'artiste. Une IA n'a pas de vécu.
[Hugo] Mais l'artiste qui utilise l'IA comme outil a un vécu ! Le prompt, les choix esthétiques, la curation — c'est une forme d'auteuriat.
[Sofia] C'est vrai pour l'artiste qui pilote l'outil. Mais qu'en est-il des œuvres 100 % autonomes, sans intervention humaine ? Là, je pense qu'on sort du domaine de l'art.
[Hugo] Peut-être. Mais la frontière est floue. Et cette flou est lui-même une richesse pour la réflexion.
[Sofia] Sur ce point-là, je suis d'accord. Ce débat nous force à redéfinir ce qu'est l'art. Et ça, c'est précieux. Merci Hugo, merci à tous.

sujet: Sport analysis: why live drama still drives radio audiences
script:
When the BBC talks about a strong start to 2026, one number jumps out for sport: BBC Radio 5 Live reached 5.4 million weekly listeners, and 5 Live Sports Extra climbed to 907,000, up 56% year on year. That is not a small seasonal bump. That is a reminder that, in a world overflowing with clips, highlights, streams and algorithm-fed content, live sport on radio still has a very specific power. It is immediate, it is communal, and it asks the audience to lean in rather than scroll past.

And that matters, because the sports landscape right now is packed. Baseball is grinding through a relentless MLB season, the PGA Championship is in full swing, and across football, golf, motorsport and beyond, there is no shortage of fixtures, storylines and pressure points. The BBC’s own schedule reflects that appetite. Sports Extra is being used exactly as it was designed to be used: as the place for extra live commentaries from major sporting events. When the action demands more airtime, the audience follows.

What’s interesting is that the growth in Sports Extra sits alongside a broader success story for speech radio. BBC Radio 4 remains the UK’s leading speech station, and the Today programme has risen during a busy period for news. That tells us something important about listening habits. People do not separate sport from the rest of the world. They want context. They want explanation. They want to understand not just what happened, but why it matters. Sport on radio works best when it feels like part of a larger conversation about pressure, performance, setbacks and momentum.

That is exactly why live commentary continues to thrive. On television or on a phone screen, you can see the play. On radio, you experience it. The gap between what is happening and what you know creates tension, and tension is the lifeblood of sport. A late save in football, a walk-off hit in baseball, a final-round charge in golf: these moments are not diminished by being heard rather than seen. In many cases, they are intensified. The imagination fills in the detail. The voice carries the drama.

Take the current baseball season. The headlines are stacking up daily: the Yankees dealing with injuries, the Dodgers waiting on pitching concerns, the Reds and Guardians locked in tight series, and the Subway Series delivering its usual edge. Those are exactly the kind of stories that radio can turn into theatre. A two-run lead in the eighth inning is one thing on a scoreboard. On air, with a commentator riding every pitch and every crowd reaction, it becomes a narrative. The listener is not just informed. The listener is inside the game.

Golf offers a different kind of radio appeal, but it is just as strong. The PGA Championship is a perfect example. Golf on radio is not about constant action. It is about rhythm, anticipation and the slow build of pressure. A player making a run on a Saturday afternoon or stumbling at the cut line is the sort of story that radio can frame beautifully. The BBC’s live coverage of the PGA Championship across its radio platforms shows an understanding that big golf events are not background noise. They are appointment listening for a dedicated audience.

There is also something deeper going on here: trust. Live sports radio is one of the few media spaces where audiences still expect expertise delivered in real time, without the filter of endless debate clips or hot-take culture. That does not mean radio is old-fashioned. It means it has retained a credibility that newer formats often struggle to sustain. Listeners know they are getting commentary, analysis and reaction from people who understand the rhythms of the game and the needs of the audience.

And the figures suggest that trust is being rewarded. BBC podcasts and on-demand radio programmes were downloaded 231 million times on third-party platforms last quarter, while audio across the BBC, including BBC Sport, reached an average weekly audience of 5.8 million, peaking at 7.2 million during the week of The Traitors: Uncloaked finale. Sport is part of that larger audio ecosystem, and it benefits from it. A fan who discovers a match through live radio may later catch up on highlights, analysis or a podcast. The relationship is no longer linear. It is a loop.

That is a crucial point for anyone thinking about the future of sports broadcasting. The old argument was that live radio would be displaced by visual platforms. But the evidence points in the opposite direction. Live sport has become more fragmented, more distributed and more on-demand than ever, yet that has made the live voice more valuable, not less. In a crowded media market, clarity wins. Radio gives you clarity. It strips away the noise and keeps the focus on the contest.

There is also an emotional dimension here that analytics can miss. Sport is not just about elite performance. It is about routine, ritual and belonging. Fans listen in cars, kitchens, workplaces and on late-night commutes. They listen because radio fits into life rather than demanding that life stop. That flexibility is part of the medium’s strength. A listener can follow a game while doing something else, but still feel fully present when the decisive moment arrives.

For BBC Radio 5 Live, the sports agenda has clearly been a key driver. The station is built around news, call-ins and live coverage, and that combination gives sport a wider frame. A major game is never just a scoreline. It is a talking point, a reaction, a controversy, a mood. That helps explain why 5 Live remains one of the BBC’s strongest speech brands. It serves both the facts and the feelings around sport.

Sports Extra, meanwhile, plays a complementary role. It is the overflow channel, yes, but that undersells it. In practice, it is the place where the BBC can deepen coverage, extend live action and serve fans who want the full event rather than a highlights package. That matters in an age when many sports are squeezed into short-form formats. Fans still want the long version. They want the spell of a whole session, a whole round, a whole match.

The 56% rise in Sports Extra’s audience is especially notable because it suggests that there is real demand for that depth. It may also reflect a simple truth: when the calendar is full, the audience expands. Major tournaments, packed news cycles and high-stakes fixtures create a habit of tuning in. Once listeners are in the habit of checking live radio for sport, they often stay.

So what does this all mean? It means sport on radio is not surviving by accident. It is succeeding because it offers something distinct. It is live without being overwhelming. It is detailed without being cluttered. It is expert without being elitist. And above all, it understands that sport is a story told in real time, not just a set of numbers after the final whistle.

As 2026 unfolds, the BBC’s audience figures suggest that the appetite for that kind of storytelling remains strong. In fact, it may be growing. In an era of endless visual content, the simple act of listening still has the power to hold attention. And when the stakes are high, that may be the most impressive result of all.

sujet: Wellness in 2026: more personal, more social, more real
script:
[Tom] Bonjour à tous, welcome back to the show. Today we’re talking lifestyle, but not in the glossy, impossible-to-copy way. We’re looking at the wellness trends shaping 2026, and honestly, it feels like the whole conversation is changing.

[Inès] Oui, exactly. For years, wellness was often sold as a solo mission: optimize yourself, track everything, improve everything, never miss a step. But the new wave is much more human. It’s less about perfection and more about what actually fits into real life.

[Tom] And that’s the key phrase, isn’t it? Real life. One of the biggest shifts we’re seeing is that people don’t want wellness to stop when they go on holiday. They want travel to support their habits, not interrupt them.

[Inès] Absolutely. According to current wellness travel trends, people are booking destinations where they can keep training, recover properly, and even learn from experts while they’re away. So instead of coming back from a trip feeling completely off-track, they come back with better routines.

[Tom] That’s a huge change. In the past, a holiday meant, “I’ll deal with health later.” Now it’s more like, “How can this trip help me feel better when I get home?”

[Inès] And the interesting part is that these trips are becoming much more personalized. Retreats are no longer just a fixed schedule of yoga at 8, smoothie at 9, meditation at 10, whether you need it or not.

[Tom] Which sounds lovely for about a day, and then maybe a little rigid.

[Inès] Exactly. In 2026, the big idea is tailoring the experience to your body, your mind, and your lifestyle. That means pre-arrival assessments, health diagnostics, and programs designed around your actual needs.

[Tom] So if you’re exhausted, maybe you don’t need a bootcamp. Maybe you need sleep, gentle movement, and proper recovery.

[Inès] Yes. And if you’re already active, you might want something more intense. Some resorts are blending high-tech treatments with sport: boxing, pilates, padel, endurance training. It’s wellness, but with a pulse.

[Tom] I love that phrase: wellness with a pulse. Because another trend is the rise of athleisure tourism and endurance travel. People are literally planning trips around physical goals.

[Inès] Marathons, destination races, Hyrox events, training camps. It’s not just about seeing a city anymore, it’s about sweating in it.

[Tom] And there’s something social about that too. You’re not just chasing a finish time. You’re sharing the effort, the pain, the victory. That’s a very different kind of memory from, say, lying by a pool in complete silence.

[Inès] Though to be fair, there is a place for both.

[Tom] Oh, absolutely. I’m not anti-pool.

[Inès] No one is. But what’s fascinating is that wellness is becoming more communal. Healing together is a major theme for 2026.

[Tom] Community as medicine, in a way.

[Inès] Exactly. Social wellness spaces are growing fast: run clubs, sauna gatherings, group ice baths, Pilates parties. Even more ritual-based experiences are gaining attention, like traditional sweat lodge ceremonies where people gather in heat, song, and shared intention.

[Tom] That’s powerful, because it reframes wellness from “What can I fix in myself?” to “What can we experience together?”

[Inès] And that shift matters. A lot of people are tired of wellness that feels lonely or performative. They want connection. They want belonging. They want something that feels grounding, not like another task on a checklist.

[Tom] Which brings us to another trend: going analogue. Digital detox is back in a big way.

[Inès] Very much so. More people are looking for ways to step away from constant screen time. That can mean dumb phones, journaling on paper, letter writing, forest bathing, guided walks in the woods.

[Tom] Forest bathing sounds very elegant.

[Inès] It does, but the idea is simple: slow down, notice your surroundings, and let your nervous system breathe for a minute.

[Tom] And that’s really where a lot of wellness is heading, isn’t it? Not just “How do I become more productive?” but “How do I feel safe, calm, and regulated?”

[Inès] Yes. Nervous-system safety is becoming a central focus. There’s a move away from hard metrics and toward emotional repair, pleasure, softness, and presence.

[Tom] That’s a real cultural pivot. For a while, wellness was almost competitive: who slept best, who tracked the most steps, who had the cleanest morning routine.

[Inès] And now the language is changing. The idea is not to optimize yourself into burnout. It’s to create habits that are sustainable and kind.

[Tom] Which makes me think of “wellness stacking,” another trend people are talking about.

[Inès] Yes, and it’s a very practical one. The idea is to pair a new habit with something you already do. Stretch while brushing your teeth, meditate while the kettle boils, drink water right after your alarm goes off.

[Tom] Simple, almost invisible changes.

[Inès] Exactly. No grand reinvention required. That’s why it works. It respects the fact that people are busy.

[Tom] And I think that’s why these trends are resonating. They’re not asking people to become different humans overnight. They’re asking people to build better rhythms.

[Inès] Well said. And even in supplementation, we’re seeing that same shift. The one-size-fits-all vitamin era is fading. People want more specialized nutrition, more testing, more tailored support.

[Tom] So the future of wellness is less “buy this miracle product” and more “understand your own body.”

[Inès] Yes, but with a healthier tone. Less obsession, more information. Less pressure, more personalization.

[Tom] I also think technology has a complicated but interesting role here. On one hand, people want to unplug. On the other hand, there’s still an appetite for smart tools, wearables, virtual experiences, even wellness in the metaverse.

[Inès] True. It’s not that tech is disappearing. It’s that people want tech to support them quietly in the background, rather than constantly demanding attention.

[Tom] That’s a very good distinction.

[Inès] Think about it: if a device can help regulate sleep or reduce stress without turning your life into a dashboard, that feels useful. But if it becomes another source of pressure, people will walk away.

[Tom] So the real luxury in 2026 may not be the most expensive spa treatment or the fanciest gadget.

[Inès] It may be calm. It may be a good night’s sleep. It may be a trip where you actually return restored. Or a community where you feel seen.

[Tom] I like that. It’s wellness that fits into life, instead of taking life over.

[Inès] And that’s probably why this moment feels so refreshing. The best wellness trends of 2026 aren’t about extremes. They’re about balance, belonging, and making healthy choices easier to keep.

[Tom] So if we had to sum it up in one sentence?

[Inès] Wellness in 2026 is more personal, more social, and much less about performance.

[Tom] I’d say that’s a trend worth keeping. Thanks for joining us, and maybe tonight, instead of one more scroll, take a walk, stretch for two minutes, or just drink a glass of water.

[Inès] Tiny habits, big impact. That’s the spirit.

[Tom] Thanks for listening, and we’ll be back soon with more lifestyle conversations that actually fit your life.

sujet: Tech & AI Debate: productivity boom or costly disruption?
script:
[Marc] Bonjour à tous, and welcome to today’s debate. The big question: is AI finally delivering the productivity revolution we were promised, or are we watching a very expensive reshuffle of jobs, power, and debt?

[Léa] And maybe the honest answer is: both. Because this week’s headlines make the picture crystal clear. On one side, we’ve got AI becoming more useful, more embedded, and more agentic. On the other, we’ve got layoffs, bond sales, security risks, and a growing sense that the bill is coming due.

[Marc] Let’s start with the optimistic case. Google is pushing Gemini deeper into Android and its ecosystem, including features like smarter dictation in Gboard, AI-native laptops, and even tools that let people create widgets through natural language. That sounds like real consumer value. Less friction, faster workflows, more people able to build useful things without coding.

[Léa] Sure, but it also sounds like a platform company making sure it owns every layer of the experience. If your phone, keyboard, laptop, and dashboard are all mediated by the same AI assistant, that’s convenience — but it’s also dependency. The more AI becomes the interface, the harder it is for competitors, and for users, to escape that ecosystem.

[Marc] But isn’t that what great technology does? It removes complexity. If someone can say, “build me a meal-prep widget” or “summarize this document,” and the device just does it, that’s not dystopia. That’s software finally becoming intuitive.

[Léa] Intuitive for whom? For users, maybe. For workers, not always. Reuters has been reporting that companies are cutting jobs while shifting investment toward AI. Meta is laying off thousands. Cisco is cutting staff. Block already moved to slash more than 4,000 roles. The message from business is pretty blunt: automation is not just about helping employees do more. It’s about needing fewer employees.

[Marc] I won’t deny the pain. But historically, new waves of technology do displace some roles while creating others. The real question is whether AI is creating enough new value fast enough to justify the disruption.

[Léa] And the answer today is uncertain. We’re seeing AI adoption accelerate, but not necessarily broad-based gain sharing. A lot of the efficiency gains are going straight to margins, stock buybacks, and infrastructure spending. Reuters also noted that tech companies are tapping debt markets to fund AI and cloud expansion. Meta has issued huge bond offerings to finance its AI buildout. That’s not a sign of cheap, effortless progress. That’s a sign of a capital race.

[Marc] Or a sign of confidence. If companies are borrowing billions, it means they believe the returns will be there. They’re betting that AI will become as essential as cloud computing, mobile, or search.

[Léa] Betting is the right word. Because the infrastructure is so expensive that even the biggest firms are stretching balance sheets. And the question becomes: who pays if the returns arrive later than expected? Shareholders? Workers? Consumers? Governments?

[Marc] Consumers may already be paying in a different way: with convenience. Anthropic moving into AI legal services, for example, suggests AI can automate document review and drafting. That could lower costs for firms and make legal help more accessible.

[Léa] Or it could hollow out entry-level legal work and make the profession even more top-heavy. Every industry says the same thing at first: “This will free people from drudgery.” Then the drudgery disappears, and so do the junior roles that trained the next generation.

[Marc] That’s a fair concern. But there’s also a real shortage of affordable expertise in many sectors. If AI can handle first-pass drafting, triage, and review, professionals can focus on judgment and strategy.

[Léa] That’s the ideal case. The practical case is messier. The more agentic AI becomes, the more we need guardrails. And this week’s news shows the tension clearly. Companies are racing to build systems that act on behalf of users, but security specialists are warning that the attack surface is expanding too. AI learning modules for secure coding, new protection modes in Android, intrusion logging for journalists and activists — all of that tells you the threat model is evolving fast.

[Marc] Absolutely. More AI means more responsibility. And that’s why I think the security story is actually encouraging. Developers are not ignoring the risks. They’re building training, logging, and protection into the platform.

[Léa] But are they doing it fast enough? The moment you let AI take actions — write code, manage workflows, shop, draft, automate — you also let it make mistakes at machine speed. That is the agentic paradox: the more capable the system becomes, the more costly its errors can be.

[Marc] Yet that same capability is why the upside is so large. Think about software development. Red Hat is bridging local and cloud environments for agentic AI development. That means prototypes can move faster into production. Secure Code Warrior is partnering with AWS to help developers build secure applications by design. Those are practical, not theoretical, gains.

[Léa] Practical, yes. But they also prove the market is reorganizing around AI whether workers want it or not. Even consumer products are changing behavior. Android’s Pause Point feature, which forces a break before opening distracting apps, is a nice reminder that AI isn’t only about productivity. It’s also about attention management, and maybe even behavioral correction.

[Marc] I actually like that. It shows tech can be designed for well-being, not just engagement.

[Léa] Maybe. Or it shows platforms are responding to the backlash they helped create.

[Marc] Let’s broaden this beyond phones and layoffs. The startup ecosystem is still funding AI aggressively. A Capital raised a $450 million fund to back AI, fintech, healthcare, and security. That tells me investors still see room for innovation at every layer.

[Léa] It tells me investors still fear missing the next wave. There’s a difference. Capital is chasing AI because nobody wants to be the person who ignored the internet in 1999. But not every AI company will survive. Not every use case is real. And not every “agent” is actually useful.

[Marc] True. We’ve seen hype cycles before. But there are also genuine breakthroughs. Meta is reportedly building a highly personalized AI assistant that can operate across software and hardware environments. Google is embedding Gemini into everyday tools. Rivian is adding an onboard AI assistant to vehicles. These are not just demos. They’re signs that AI is becoming ambient.

[Léa] Ambient is another word for unavoidable. And once AI is everywhere, regulation becomes unavoidable too. California’s AI transparency rules, the EU AI Act, disclosure requirements, labeling, provenance — all of that is moving from theory to practice. The more AI-generated content floods the internet, the more people need to know what’s synthetic, what’s assisted, and what’s real.

[Marc] And that’s healthy. Regulation can build trust.

[Léa] If it’s done well. If it’s too slow, or too fragmented, it just creates compliance theater. But at least policymakers are starting to treat AI as infrastructure, not just a novelty.

[Marc] So where does that leave us? I’d argue AI in 2026 is entering its utility era. It’s less about flashy chatbots and more about embedded assistance: coding, legal drafting, transport, productivity, security, and device interaction. The technology is finally starting to feel operational.

[Léa] And I’d argue it’s also entering its accountability era. Because once AI becomes operational, the costs stop being abstract. They show up in payrolls, debt, outages, errors, lawsuits, and public trust. The winners will be the companies that can prove AI is not just intelligent, but reliable, affordable, and responsible.

[Marc] So maybe the real debate isn’t whether AI is good or bad.

[Léa] Exactly. It’s whether we’re building an economy where the gains are shared, the risks are managed, and the humans stay in control.

[Marc] That’s the challenge. Not to slow the technology down, but to make sure progress doesn’t become a synonym for disruption.

[Léa] And that’s where this story gets interesting. Because AI is no longer coming. It’s here. The only question now is: who benefits, who adapts, and who gets left behind?

[Marc] That’s all for today’s debate. Stay with us — because the next chapter of AI won’t be written by the machines alone.

[Léa] It’ll be written by the choices we make around them.

sujet:
International News Daily Recap
Claire’s roundup of the day’s top global headlines
script:
Good evening, I’m Claire, and this is your daily international news recap.

We begin in West Africa, where President Trump says a top Islamic State leader has been killed in a joint U.S.-Nigerian operation. In a late-night social media post, Trump said Abu Bakr al-Mainuki, whom he described as the group’s second-in-command globally, was taken out in the mission. Nigerian authorities have not yet publicly laid out all the details, but the announcement points to a major counterterrorism development in a region that has battled extremist violence for years. For communities across Nigeria and neighboring countries, any strike against a figure this high up is likely to be seen as significant, even as questions remain about the operation itself and what comes next.

In the Middle East, Israel says it has killed the leader of Hamas’ military wing, a man it describes as one of the architects of the October 7th, 2023 attacks that ignited the war in Gaza. The announcement comes amid continuing fighting and deepening humanitarian strain. Palestinian civilians in Gaza remain caught in the middle of the conflict, with reports of strikes and mounting destruction. The war has now dragged on long enough to reshape politics, public opinion, and daily life across the region. Any claim of a high-level assassination is likely to reverberate far beyond the battlefield, potentially affecting negotiations, retaliatory attacks, and the already fragile hope for a broader ceasefire.

And while the fighting continues, so does the wider diplomatic standoff around Iran. Iran’s foreign minister says a lack of trust remains the biggest obstacle in negotiations with the United States over the country’s nuclear program. That trust gap has long been at the center of talks, but it has only widened amid regional conflict, sanctions pressure, and sharp rhetoric from Washington and Tehran. The stakes are enormous: the future of Iran’s nuclear program, the possibility of renewed sanctions relief, and the risk of another escalation in the Middle East all hang in the balance. Even when both sides say they want progress, the path forward remains narrow and uncertain.

Turning to Europe, the Eurovision Song Contest is reaching its grand final in Vienna, and as always, the spectacle is about more than music. The contest is under tight security, and rainy weather has not dampened fan enthusiasm. But this year’s event is also drawing protests from critics who say Israel should not be invited. Eurovision has long billed itself as a celebration of pop, performance, and cross-border unity, but it often becomes a stage for political tension as well. That tension is especially visible in 2026, with the contest taking place against the backdrop of war in the Middle East and polarized debate across Europe. Still, for many fans, the final remains a rare moment of shared excitement, glitter, and pure showmanship.

Eurovision has always thrived on contrast: the serious and the absurd, the artistic and the political, the carefully choreographed and the gloriously unpredictable. This year’s grand final promises all of that again. And for one night, at least, millions of viewers will tune in not just to watch who wins, but to see which performance, costume, or surprise moment becomes the one everyone talks about tomorrow.

In another European story that shows how politics can reach into everyday life, a small Bavarian town is reacting to President Trump’s threat to withdraw U.S. troops from Germany. The town depends heavily on American military personnel and their families, not just for income but for friendships that have developed over decades. That makes the possibility of a withdrawal feel less like a distant strategic adjustment and more like a direct blow to local life. Across Germany, U.S. bases have long been part of the social and economic fabric in certain communities. Now, residents are being forced to confront the possibility that a major piece of that fabric could be torn away.

And in Britain, politics is once again being shaped by the far right. Supporters of activist Tommy Robinson have taken part in a “Unite the Kingdom” march in London, highlighting how identity, immigration, and nationalism continue to fuel street politics in the U.K. These rallies often draw strong reactions in both directions, with supporters framing them as a defense of free speech and national values, while critics warn they normalize exclusion and extremism. In a country already wrestling with economic pressure and political fragmentation, such marches are another reminder of how volatile the public mood remains.

Elsewhere in Europe, there’s a very different kind of headline-making conversation: culture. The BBC is highlighting a new adaptation of Lord of the Flies, with writer Jack Thorne bringing William Golding’s classic study of hate and division to a world that feels, in many ways, even more divided than before. It’s a reminder that stories written decades ago can still speak directly to the present. And that may be why so many of the arts and culture stories making headlines this week feel unexpectedly political. Even a painting, a TV series, or a song contest can become a mirror for the anxieties of the moment.

From Europe, we move to the Americas, where the political and economic consequences of policy are front and center. In Oregon, a gas tax increase passed by Democrats is now heading to voters in a referendum, with affordability concerns complicating the state’s broader message. As fuel prices remain a sensitive issue for households, the debate over taxes has become a test of how much pain voters are willing to accept in the name of infrastructure, climate goals, or public investment. It’s a familiar story in modern politics: governments argue for long-term benefits while voters feel the short-term pinch at the pump.

And in the United States, those affordability pressures are part of a much larger national conversation. Americans are still grappling with the cost of everyday life, while politicians debate everything from taxes to trade to energy. Those debates may feel domestic, but they ripple outward. Trade policy affects global supply chains. Energy policy affects international markets. And decisions made in Washington can quickly become talking points in capitals around the world.

In Asia, a string of headlines shows just how interconnected the region remains. A freight train crash in Bangkok killed at least eight people after plowing into traffic on train tracks and sparking a fire. Authorities have not yet fully explained the cause, though they say a barrier had not come down. It is the kind of disaster that exposes how a single breakdown in infrastructure can have deadly consequences in a crowded city. As investigators work to determine what went wrong, families are left mourning, and officials are under pressure to answer basic questions about safety and maintenance.

Meanwhile, the BBC is also drawing attention to the rising popularity and value of prized Chinese green tea, with some varieties reportedly costing as much as $8,800 per kilogram. That kind of price tag says as much about status and rarity as it does about flavor. Across Asia, luxury food and drink markets continue to expand, feeding a global appetite for exclusivity. Whether it’s tea, cars, or technology, consumer culture in the region remains a major force shaping international trends.

And speaking of technology, the BBC is also spotlighting the systems powering the future of driving, along with the boldest new cars unveiled at the Beijing Auto Show. Auto makers are racing to position themselves for an era defined by electric vehicles, smarter software, and more advanced driver assistance. The competition is no longer just about horsepower or design. It’s about who can build the safest, smartest, and most connected vehicle for the next generation of roads.

In South America, political currents continue to shift as Venezuela remains under intense scrutiny. Images out of Caracas show a mural of ousted President Nicolás Maduro, a reminder that even after years of crisis, the country’s political future remains unsettled. Venezuela’s economy, institutions, and international relationships have all been strained by prolonged instability. For many Venezuelans, the question is no longer just who governs, but whether the country can find a path toward basic economic recovery and political normalcy.

And in Cuba, attention is turning toward the powerful conglomerate that controls much of the economy. The New York Times is reporting on the secretive structure behind that system, which underscores how deeply state-linked business interests shape life on the island. In Cuba, as in so many places, economics and politics are inseparable. Control of key industries often means control of opportunity, mobility, and reform itself.

There’s also a sobering climate story this week. Reuters reports that global fire outbreaks have hit a record high, with climate change driving unprecedented fire activity across Africa, Asia, and beyond. Experts warn conditions are likely to worsen. That means more danger for communities, more strain on emergency services, and more destruction of land and livelihoods. Wildfires are no longer just a seasonal hazard in a few regions; they are becoming a global climate emergency, linked to heat, drought, and shifting weather patterns. The scale of the crisis is forcing governments to rethink preparedness, land management, and adaptation.

And in the background of all these headlines, another broader pattern is impossible to ignore: war, climate stress, and political instability are increasingly reinforcing one another. Conflicts displace people, strain resources, and distract governments from long-term planning. Climate shocks deepen inequality and fuel migration pressures. And political extremism thrives where institutions are weakest. That’s the world this news cycle reflects — not one crisis, but many, overlapping and accelerating.

Still, not every headline is grim. Eurovision reminds us that people still gather for spectacle and song. Culture stories remind us that art still asks big questions. And even in the most difficult regions, diplomacy continues — however imperfectly — because the alternative is often worse.

So that’s your international roundup for today: a major counterterrorism claim in Nigeria, a new escalation in the Gaza war, stalled nuclear talks with Iran, Eurovision under the spotlight in Vienna, troop worries in Germany, political tensions in Britain, a deadly train crash in Bangkok, climate-fueled fire outbreaks, and a global picture that remains as complicated as ever.

I’m Claire. Thanks for listening, and I’ll be back with more updates in the next recap.
